Part B.key_evidence: Single detection (ndethist=1, deltajd=0) at a sky location that has been visited 1559 times without prior detections. Positive-flux PSF source at mag 17.55 in g-band. No close PS1 counterpart (distpsnr1=10.2"), and the nearest PS1 source is faint (r~21.4) and galaxy-like (sgscore1~0.007). classtar=0.98 indicates point-like morphology. Reference cutout shows no persistent source at the center. Science/difference cutouts are noisy but the candidate appears as a compact positive residual with no visible host.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a solar-system object (asteroid): a one-off, point-like, positive-flux detection at a well-covered field with no underlying host galaxy and no previous detections over many epochs. The lack of any coincident PS1 or reference source, combined with the isolated single-epoch appearance, favors a moving object caught in one exposure.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A supernova is possible—an isolated point source with no bright host could be a hostless SN or one in a very faint galaxy below PS1 depth. However, with only a single detection, no visible host in the reference, and deltajd=0, the SN scenario is less supported than a transient solar-system crossing, especially given the 1559 prior visits to this position without detection.
